ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic snail food product class; no specific entry for Cerithidea obtusa or Malay masak lemak preparation exists. Closest defensible anchor used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW covers cooked snails generically; no specific code for masak lemak preparation or Cerithidea obtusa species. Closest defensible code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Malay shellfish dish. Left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Siput Sedut Masak Lemak is approximately 200 g, representing a communal side-dish portion eaten alongside steamed white rice.

nutrient_source.title: Nutrient Composition of Malaysian Foods (IMR Malaysia MyFCD) — shellfish and mollusc dishes; supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for snails (cooked) and coconut milk for composite estimation
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research Malaysia (IMR) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Siput Sedut Masak Lemak exists in IMR MyFCD or USDA FoodData Central. Values are adapted from IMR MyFCD shellfish-in-coconut-gravy analogues and USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked snails (FDC ID 175177) and coconut milk (FDC ID 169411), weighted to reflect the dish composition (approximately 55% snail meat, 35% coconut gravy, 10% aromatics). Input AI-generated values were cross-checked against these sources and retained where consistent. Saturated fat estimated from coconut milk fat fraction (approximately 85% of total fat is saturated in coconut milk). Cholesterol estimated from USDA cooked snail data.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — analogous estimate based on shellfish and high-protein low-carbohydrate cooked dishes; University of Sydney GI Database reference for high-protein seafood dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Siput Sedut Masak Lemak. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on: (1) high protein content of snail meat (which attenuates postprandial glucose response), (2) high saturated fat from coconut milk (which further slows gastric emptying and carbohydrate absorption), and (3) low net carbohydrate content (4.8 g/100g). This is consistent with GI values reported for high-protein, high-fat shellfish dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (35 × 9.6 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 3.4, classified as low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (280 mg/100g) and high saturated fat from coconut milk
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150 g and avoid frequent consumption, as the combined sodium load (560 mg per 200 g serving) and high saturated fat content from coconut milk may elevate blood pressure and contribute to arterial stiffness.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (estimated 35) and high protein content (8.5 g/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Siput Sedut Masak Lemak has a low estimated GI and high protein content, making it a relatively suitable side dish for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, the calorie-dense coconut milk gravy should be accounted for in total daily energy intake, and the dish should be paired with a controlled portion of rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat from coconut milk (estimated 7.8 g saturated fat per 100g) and moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high saturated fat content from coconut milk (approximately 76% of total fat) may raise LDL cholesterol with regular consumption; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit intake to occasional servings and request reduced-coconut-milk preparations where possible.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory compounds from turmeric (curcumin) and capsaicin from bird's eye chili
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Turmeric and bird's eye chili in the dish provide curcumin and capsaicin with documented anti-inflammatory properties; however, the high saturated fat content may be pro-inflammatory in some individuals and should be monitored.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Siput Sedut Masak Lemak (also known as Siput Sedut Masak Lemak Cili Api) is a traditional Malay shellfish dish made from mud snails (Cerithidea obtusa), a species of mangrove-dwelling mollusc harvested from coastal mudflats and mangrove forests across Malaysia, Singapore, and Indonesia. The snails are prepared by snipping off their pointed tails before cooking, which allows diners to suck (sedut in Malay) the tender snail meat and the rich, fragrant gravy directly through the shell — a defining and communal eating experience. The dish is cooked by simmering the snails in a vibrant yellow coconut milk gravy (lemak) seasoned with fresh turmeric, bird's eye chilies (cili padi), lemongrass, galangal, and shallots. The result is a deeply aromatic, mildly spicy, and richly flavoured dish with a creamy, slightly thick gravy. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 145 kcal, 8.5 g protein, 10.2 g fat (predominantly saturated fat from coconut milk at approximately 7.8 g), and 4.8 g carbohydrate. It is a meaningful source of iron (3.5 mg), calcium (110 mg), vitamin B12 (2.5 mcg), and selenium (15 mcg), reflecting the mineral-rich profile of mollusc meat. The estimated glycemic index is low (approximately 35), making it suitable for blood glucose management when consumed in controlled portions. Clinically, the high saturated fat and moderate sodium content (280 mg/100g) warrant caution for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is typically served as a side dish alongside steamed white rice at lunch or dinner and is a celebrated part of Malay culinary heritage.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Siput Sedut Masak Lemak exists in IMR MyFCD or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from IMR MyFCD shellfish-in-coconut-gravy analogues and USDA SR Legacy cooked snail and coconut milk entries. The AI-generated input values were cross-checked and found broadly consistent with these analogues. GI is an analogous estimate. Tamil and Hindi local names are descriptive translations provided for multilingual coverage as no established standard names appear in approved sources. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from IMR Malaysia MyFCD shellfish-in-coconut-gravy analogues and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked snails (FDC ID 175177) and coconut milk (FDC ID 169411); no exact published entry for this dish was found in any approved source. 2. Saturated fat (7.8 g/100g) is estimated from the coconut milk fat fraction (approximately 85% saturated). Monounsaturated fat (0.9 g) and polyunsaturated fat (0.4 g) are estimated from the same composite. Trans fat is 0.0 g (confirmed negligible in natural coconut milk and snail meat). 3. Cholesterol (45 mg/100g) is estimated from USDA SR Legacy cooked snail data. 4. water_g is set to -1: moisture data for this specific dish preparation is not available in IMR MyFCD or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-17. 5. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of 2026-04-17. 6. The input dietary_tags included 'vegetarian' which is incorrect — this dish contains snail meat (mollusc) and has been corrected to is_vegetarian: false. 7. Chinese names (椰浆辣炒泥螺 / 椰漿辣炒泥螺) are descriptive translations; no established standard Chinese name for this dish was found in approved sources. 8. Tamil local names (தேங்காய் பால் நத்தை குழம்பு — coconut milk snail curry; நத்தை மசாலா — snail masala) are descriptive translations provided for multilingual retrieval coverage; no established Tamil name for this specific Malay dish was found in approved sources. 9. Hindi local names (नारियल दूध में पकाए घोंघे — snails cooked in coconut milk; नारियल करी घोंघे — coconut curry snails) are descriptive translations provided for multilingual retrieval coverage; no established Hindi name for this specific Malay dish was found in approved sources. 10. Indonesian local name added as 'Siput Sedut Masak Lemak' as it is a common name for this dish in Indonesia. 11. Glycemic load per 200 g serving calculated as (35 × 9.6 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 3.4 (low GL). 12. All strings in aliases_by_language verified to appear verbatim in flat aliases array — self-check passed.
